This is David again. You know, our total off-premise was in line with Q3 at 23%, which is where we finished the quarter. And that mix is very, very stable. It remains about 40% of it being delivery, 35% is still phone or guests who are walking up, and then about 25% are guests who are using our online ordering platform. So I think from the beginning, we've said that we feel that our offering really continues to work well on the off-premise channel. whether that's the menu variety and the variety of price points, along with the quality that our ops team has been able to continue to deliver. So we don't think that that is going to change as we move into the new year. We've seen it be very stable in the new year and feel great about the off-premise business.", "speaker": "David Gordon", "title": "President"}, {"role": "answer", "text": "Etienne Marcus [2173]: Thank you. Our next question comes from Brian Harbor with Morgan Stanley.", "speaker": "Etienne Marcus", "title": "Vice President of Investor Relations"}]

[{"role": "question", "text": "Brian Harbor [2184]: Yeah, thank you. Good afternoon. It's a smaller piece of the movie. Could you just comment on some of the other FRC brands and how those have been performing just from a sales perspective and also, you know, which ones you think will be kind of, you know, can be bigger perhaps over time?", "speaker": "Brian Harbor", "title": "Analyst, Morgan Stanley"}, {"role": "answer", "text": "David Gordon [2200]: Sure. Well, I think as we stated before, our plans for North Italia continue to be about a 20% unit growth. And North continues, as you can see from the sales that we reported, to be very, very strong. Consumer demand is great. As we moved into new geographies, it continues to be accepted by those guests very, very well. So we feel great about the continued growth at North. Flower Child is now up to 30 restaurants across the country and moving into some new geographies as well and having that same success on the sales side. So we'd anticipate that Flower Child is going to be the next concept that we will focus bring under our umbrella a little more closely, and help continue to launch nationally. We're excited to get that going this year. There'll be three to four Flower Child this year, and that total number of 10 restaurants that FRC will be opening. And then past that, there's some other concepts like culinary dropout that we're very, very excited about. Blanco is a Mexican concept that we like a lot. We'll continue to evaluate the other FRC concepts to make sure we feel like they can have a national presence and make sure that they're hitting the margin profile that we need before we decide that we would scale them up and try and grow them the way that we are for North and for Flower Child.", "speaker": "David Gordon", "title": "President"}]

[{"role": "question", "text": "Brian Harbor [2284]: Okay, thanks. What were the asset impairments related to, or I guess, you know, how much of that was lease impairment and how much of it was other types of impairment? What was that from?", "speaker": "Brian Harbor", "title": "Analyst, Morgan Stanley"}, {"role": "answer", "text": "Matt Clark [2294]: yeah this is matt it's it's predominantly uh lease impairment and essentially you know we we hit the the the covet pandemic and there were um several urban locations that while we continue to operate and are recovering haven't fully recovered right and so this is an accounting exercise that you have to go through and the real driver today, which is just weird compared to five years ago. If you can remember back when the lease accounting changed, right? And so we actually had to gross up the balance sheet with the right of use assets. So that number, I mean, over 50% of it is just the lease side of it, not even the investments that were made.", "speaker": "Matt Clark", "title": "Executive Vice President and Chief Financial Officer"}, {"role": "answer", "text": "Etienne Marcus [2339]: Thank you. Our next question comes from Nick Setian with Wedbush Securities.", "speaker": "Etienne Marcus", "title": "Vice President of Investor Relations"}]

[{"role": "question", "text": "Michael (on behalf of Nick Setian) [2349]: Hi, this is actually Michael on for Nick. Just wondering what labor expense and other OpEx would have been as a percent of sales if you guys were to back out that extra week?", "speaker": "Michael (on behalf of Nick Setian)", "title": "Analyst, Wedbush Securities"}, {"role": "answer", "text": "Matt Clark [2361]: Well, Michael is mad to be pretty darn much the same because, you know, those are fully accrued and we contract the hours worked by week. And so, you know, those actually go hand in hand with the weeks that we operate in. So shouldn't shouldn't have been too much different. A little bit is probably slightly better just because it was a bigger week relative to the rest of the quarter, but it wasn't meaningful.", "speaker": "Matt Clark", "title": "Executive Vice President and Chief Financial Officer"}, {"role": "answer", "text": "Etienne Marcus [2389]: Got it. Thanks very much. Our next question comes from Lauren Silberman with Credit Suisse.", "speaker": "Etienne Marcus", "title": "Vice President of Investor Relations"}]

[{"role": "question", "text": "Lauren Silberman [2399]: Thank you very much. Just on comp, obviously really strong trans quarter to date. What's your sense of what's driving the underlying acceleration broadly? And looking versus 19, are you seeing relative consistency week to week over the quarter date period?", "speaker": "Lauren Silberman", "title": "Analyst, Credit Suisse"}, {"role": "answer", "text": "Matt Clark [2414]: Hi, Lauren, it's Matt. Yeah, you know, I think that's a good metric. We like to balance it, like I said, between 2019 and year over year. And it's been very stable against the 2019. I think we're seeing um maybe maybe a return to more normalcy in terms of seasonality patterns if you will um i think there was a little bit of talk in in january about some positive weather um i don't know uh certainly a little bit of the strength in the first week or two was was the omicron lapping on a year over year but that wouldn't account for the 2019 data right which was we think that's important to look at. I just think in general, we're seeing a normalization of the consumer trends and our brands resonate. And so the utilization is similar to what we might have expected pre-pandemic.", "speaker": "Matt Clark", "title": "Executive Vice President and Chief Financial Officer"}]

[{"role": "question", "text": "Lauren Silberman [2470]: Great. Thank you for that. And then just another follow-up on consumer behavior. You mentioned that there's no real signs in sort of tech management or attachment rates. Also doing, I think it's the 45th anniversary weekday promotion. Is this in any response to what you're seeing? Just trying to understand if there's any type of shift that is discernible.", "speaker": "Lauren Silberman", "title": "Analyst, Credit Suisse"}, {"role": "answer", "text": "Matt Clark [2491]: Lauren, no, this is Matt. No, we do the marketing and that type of offer is really off-premise driven. And we do that on occasion. I think Frankly, it would have been the opposite, right, because what we're seeing are really strong trends, which would say, oh, you know, maybe you don't need to do the offers, but I think we just like to keep a regular cadence, remind the guests. I think particularly in the off-channels, off-promise channels, staying a little bit more top of mind because there's so much promotional activity, I think that that's important, you know, regardless of whether we have strong trends right now, which we do or not.", "speaker": "Matt Clark", "title": "Executive Vice President and Chief Financial Officer"}, {"role": "answer", "text": "David Gordon [2533]: And certainly celebrating the 45th anniversary garners us additional PR. We've always had very strong concept PR, and there's just another way for people to celebrate the anniversary with us.", "speaker": "David Gordon", "title": "President"}]

[{"role": "question", "text": "Lauren Silberman [2546]: Great. Thank you, guys.", "speaker": "Lauren Silberman", "title": "Analyst, Credit Suisse"}, {"role": "answer", "text": "Etienne Marcus [2549]: Our next question comes from Jeffrey Bernstein with Barclays.", "speaker": "Etienne Marcus", "title": "Vice President of Investor Relations"}]

[{"role": "question", "text": "Jeffrey Bernstein [2556]: Great. Thank you very much. Two things. First, just as we think about the 2023 margin, I think you said the restaurant margin should be at that 15% level for the overall corporate. But I know in the past year I talked about operating margins. I think you had said you were looking back to 19 and you wanted to get them back to that 5.2% range, seemingly G&A being the big driver between the restaurant and the operating margin. So I'm just wondering how we should think about that operating margin and whether on G&A, whether you look at it on dollar spend or growth relative to revenues, how should we think about that in 23? Another one follow-up.", "speaker": "Jeffrey Bernstein", "title": "Analyst, Barclays"}, {"role": "answer", "text": "Matt Clark [2593]: Sure. Jeff, this is Matt. I think generally all that still holds, right? I mean, when I look at sort of what's out there in terms of the estimates and expectations, it pretty much surrounds that general approach. I mean, our goal with G&A is to continue to move it equal or better as a percentage of sales every year, right, until we get to that 6% level. I think the other pieces kind of below the four-wall level You know, depreciation would probably be pretty similar year over year. Pre-opening will be up a little bit year over year. So I think those are kind of where things would shake out.", "speaker": "Matt Clark", "title": "Executive Vice President and Chief Financial Officer"}]

[{"role": "question", "text": "Jeffrey Bernstein [2632]: Got it. And then just on the value scores, I think you mentioned that your pricing is 400 basis points below food away from home. And I know you often note being better insulated because presumably you target a slightly higher income consumer, at least at your core brand. I know there are some that are concerned that maybe the higher income might be more vulnerable based on the current factors at play here. I'm just wondering how you measure your value scores and determine price above and beyond kind of the government data. Is there testing that you do or survey work? Just trying to get a measure for your confidence that your value scores hold up in the event of a slowing macro. It's a little bit art, a little bit science-based. I think in addition to the government data, we track our peer set. I've said this many times, but it's true. We look at 50 different markets, two dozen national competitors, and we try to assess whether or not we're either at or slightly below the midpoint there. From an alternative approach for consumers to be looking at, we're taking no more if not less pricing than the competitors, which I guess goes hand-in-hand with the government data, right, because these are the biggest chains. I think we look at price elasticity, so we do our own internal analytical evaluation, and we evaluate whether we're seeing trade-off in market basket or not and check attachment. And I think, as we've said so far, we're seeing very consistent patterns there as well. And we do do research, qualitative research, so we're constantly monitoring what our guests are saying about our value and making sure that that score is consistent. I think the last thing I would say is that if the \u2013 top end of consumers really does feel pressure. We're likely to actually benefit some from that because they typically move down off of the steakhouses and the expensive independents, the one-offs, into Cheesecake Factory and into our brands because we're still very, very accessible.
